name: Notice and Choice Principle
summary: A data user shall, before collecting personal data about a data subject, inform the data subject in writing of the matters set out in Section 7(1) and provide the data subject with a choice to decide whether to allow processing of the data for purposes beyond those directly required for the transaction. This principle underpins the transparency and autonomy of data subjects under the PDPA.

obligations.obligation: Provide the data subject with written notice before or at the time of collection of personal data
Include in the notice a description of the personal data being collected and the purposes for which it will be processed
Disclose in the notice the classes of third parties to whom the personal data may be transferred or disclosed
Inform the data subject of their right to request access to and correction of their personal data, and the contact details for submitting such requests
Provide the notice in both Bahasa Malaysia and English
Provide the data subject with a choice to opt out of having their personal data processed for purposes beyond the primary purpose of the collection, including direct marketing
Inform the data subject whether it is obligatory or voluntary to supply the personal data, and where it is obligatory, the consequences of failing to provide the data

exceptions.exception: Notice is not required where it would involve a disproportionate effort relative to the purposes for which the data was collected
exceptions.conditions: The data user must document why providing notice would involve disproportionate effort; this exception cannot be used merely because notice would be inconvenient; it is most applicable to processing of publicly available data or historical records

exceptions.exception: Notice is not required where the recording or disclosure of personal data is expressly required or authorised by or under any law
exceptions.conditions: The specific law or regulation authorising or requiring the data collection must be identified; the exception covers only the scope of data collection authorised by that law

exceptions.exception: Notice is not required where the data subject already has the information described in Section 7(1)
exceptions.conditions: The data user bears the burden of demonstrating that the data subject had actual prior knowledge of all the required notice elements; a general awareness of data processing is insufficient

practicalGuidance: Organizations should develop a layered privacy notice strategy: a short notice at the point of collection summarising key information, with a link or reference to a comprehensive privacy policy containing full details. The notice must be written in plain language accessible to the target audience — legalese and overly technical language undermines transparency. Both a Bahasa Malaysia and an English version are legally required; translation must be accurate and the content of both versions must be substantively identical. For online data collection, a link to the privacy notice embedded in a registration or checkout form is the minimum; the data subject should be required to affirmatively acknowledge receipt. Where data is collected indirectly (e.g., from a third party), notice must still be given to the data subject as soon as reasonably practicable after collection. The 2024 Amendment tightened the notice requirements by making it mandatory to disclose whether data provision is obligatory or voluntary, and to explain the consequences of non-provision.
commonViolations: Providing a privacy notice only in English without a Bahasa Malaysia version
Publishing a privacy policy on a website without ensuring data subjects are directed to or made aware of it at the point of collection
Failing to update the privacy notice when new processing purposes or third-party disclosures are introduced
Omitting information about data subject rights or the contact details for submitting access and correction requests
Using a single blanket consent that covers marketing, analytics, and third-party sharing without giving the data subject a genuine choice to opt out of non-essential processing
ageThresholdNote: s40 of the PDPA (as amended by A1699) defines a child as a person under 18 years of age. The General Code of Practice 2022 references age 13 in the context of simplified notice requirements for online services. These are separate thresholds serving different purposes — s40 sets the general child definition for purposes of the Act (including consent by or on behalf of minors), while the General Code provides operational guidance for specific notice formats applicable to online services targeting younger users. Organisations collecting personal data from minors should apply both: the s40 child definition for determining when parental consent is required, and the General Code age-13 notice simplification for online service notice format decisions.
